hero.heading: 🐳 Docker - Ship Your Code Anywhere
hero.subtitle: Package your application with everything it needs. Build once, run everywhere without surprises.
hero.whyLearn: Docker is used by Netflix, Uber, Spotify, and Amazon. It solves the 'works on my machine but fails in production' problem once and for all.

storytelling.problem: You built an app on your Windows laptop. It works perfectly. You send it to your friend who uses Mac, and it breaks. You push it to the Linux server in production, and it crashes again. Why? Different systems have different libraries, versions, and configurations.
storytelling.solution: Docker packages your entire application—code, libraries, dependencies, and configuration—into a lightweight, portable container. This container runs identically on your laptop, your friend's Mac, or a production server in the cloud.
storytelling.analogy: Think of Docker like shipping cargo. Instead of putting your app in a box hoping the destination has the right tools to open it, Docker is like a sealed shipping container that contains everything inside—the app AND all tools needed to run it. When it arrives, everything works immediately.
storytelling.realWorldExample: Netflix streams to millions. Every time they update their service, they use Docker to package updates, test them, and deploy to thousands of servers worldwide—all identically. No surprises, no crashes.
storytelling.deepDive: Under the hood, Docker uses Linux containers (LXC) to isolate applications. Unlike VMs that simulate an entire OS (heavy!), containers share the host OS kernel but isolate filesystem and processes (light!). This makes Docker fast, efficient, and perfect for microservices.

architectureFlow.title: Write Dockerfile
architectureFlow.description: Create a recipe for your app (what base image, what to install, how to run it)
architectureFlow.details: A Dockerfile is like a blueprint. It defines the base OS, installs dependencies, copies your code, and specifies the startup command.

architectureFlow.title: Build Image
architectureFlow.description: Docker reads the Dockerfile and creates a reusable image (a snapshot of your app with everything)
architectureFlow.details: The image is a read-only template. It's not running yet—it's just a package ready to be deployed.

architectureFlow.title: Push to Registry
architectureFlow.description: Upload your image to Docker Hub or a private registry (like Amazon ECR or GitHub Packages)
architectureFlow.details: The registry is like a library. Anyone can pull your image and run it instantly, anywhere in the world.

architectureFlow.title: Run Container
architectureFlow.description: Docker spins up a running instance of your image. Your app is now live!
architectureFlow.details: A container is a live instance. You can run multiple containers from the same image, and they're all isolated.

beginner.description: Images are blueprints; containers are running instances. Like a class and an object in programming.
FROM, RUN, COPY, WORKDIR, EXPOSE, CMD—the essential commands to write a Dockerfile.
docker build, docker run, docker ps, docker logs—commands you'll use 80% of the time.
How to expose your container's port so you can access your app (-p 8080:3000 means localhost:8080 → container:3000).
How to mount directories so your data survives when containers are deleted.

intermediate.description: Run multi-container apps (frontend + database) with a single docker-compose.yml file.
Connect multiple containers so they talk to each other (app container → database container).
Understand layer caching to speed up builds and reduce image size.
Pass configuration to containers without hardcoding secrets (-e NODE_ENV=production).
Bridge, host, overlay—different network types for different use cases.

advanced.description: Create lightweight production images by separating build and runtime stages.
Faster, smarter image building with parallel stages and caching.
Run containers without root privileges for better security.
Sign images, scan for vulnerabilities, manage access control.
Resource limits, CPU/memory tuning, monitoring container metrics.

intro.what: Docker is an open-source platform for automating the deployment, scaling, and management of applications inside lightweight software containers.
intro.why: Containers provide consistent runtime environments across development, test, and production. Docker standardizes packaging of applications, dependencies, and configuration into portable images.
problems_solved: Inconsistent builds across developer machines and CI
Difficulties in packaging and deploying microservices
Inefficient use of host resources compared to VMs
intro.industry_importance: Docker is foundational to modern cloud-native architectures, powering CI/CD pipelines, microservices deployments, local developer productivity, and multi-cloud portability.

architecture.workflow: Developers write a Dockerfile that describes how to build an image. BuildKit builds the image layers and stores them in the local image store. Images are pushed to a registry. Runtimes pull images and run containers isolated by namespaces and cgroups.
architecture.request_flow: User runs 'docker run' -> CLI talks to dockerd -> dockerd uses containerd to create container -> kernel namespaces and cgroups isolate process and resources -> container process starts.
architecture.production_architecture: Images stored in a hardened registry, scanned for vulnerabilities, pulled by orchestrators (Kubernetes, ECS). Use immutable tags, image promotion workflows, signed image artifacts.
architecture.scaling_architecture: Stateless containers scaled horizontally behind load balancers; persistent storage via volumes or external managed services; use orchestration (Kubernetes/ECS) for scheduling and autoscaling.

enterprise_examples: CI pipelines: build/test containers for each merge request
Microservices: each service packaged into its own container and deployed via orchestrator
Platform engineering: standardized base images for developer teams
startup_examples: Deploy single-node containerized apps on cloud VMs
production_scenarios: Blue/Green or Canary deployments with container images
Image promotion through CI -> registry -> staging -> production

windows: Enable WSL2 backend for best performance on Windows 10/11
linux: Use official repo: curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh; sh get-docker.sh
Or use your distro package manager from official Docker repo
ubuntu: sudo apt install apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl gnupg lsb-release -y
sudo apt update && sudo apt install docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io -y
macos: Install Docker Desktop for Mac from https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop
Enable virtualization and allocate resources in Docker Desktop preferences
cloud_setup: Use managed container registry services (ECR, GCR, ACR) for storing images
Harden registries with IAM and image scanning

commands.best_practice: Pin version in CI images for repeatability
Run as a user in docker group carefully; prefer rootless where possible
Avoid using 'latest' in production; use immutable tags

commands.explanation: Build image using Dockerfile in current directory
commands.best_practice: Use multi-stage builds to keep images small

commands.use_case: Identify large images to clean up
commands.best_practice: Regularly prune unused images in CI runners

commands.explanation: Run container in detached mode mapping ports
commands.best_practice: Avoid exposing host ports in production; use service discovery
Use labels to add metadata for easier filtering
Ship logs to centralized logging instead of relying on 'docker logs' in production
Minimize reliance on exec for debugging; prefer reproducing issues with local builds

commands.explanation: Create new image from container state
commands.best_practice: Use sparingly; prefer Dockerfile-driven reproducible builds
Use signed images for security in air-gapped flows
Verify image checksums after transfer
Use canonical registry paths and consistent naming conventions
Use CI service accounts and short-lived credentials
Be cautious when removing images referenced by multiple tags
Use 'docker rm -f' when necessary but prefer graceful shutdown
Use user-defined bridge networks for service isolation

commands.use_case: Enable container name resolution and isolation
commands.best_practice: Use explicit subnets when integrating with host networks
Prefer managed storage in production; use volumes for local dev and edge cases

commands.use_case: Debug networking, mounts, and environment variables
commands.best_practice: Use 'docker inspect --format' to extract needed fields
Integrate metrics with Prometheus instead of relying on docker stats in production

commands.use_case: Understand image and volume disk usage
commands.best_practice: Regular maintenance of build agents to avoid disk exhaustion
Run with caution; prefer targeted prune in CI
Use Compose v2/BuildKit for faster builds
Use during reproducible CI builds when necessary

commands.explanation: Run tests inside a disposable container
commands.best_practice: Use consistent node image versions across CI and dev

commands.explanation: Run vulnerability scan using Docker scan (Snyk)
commands.best_practice: Fail CI on critical vulnerabilities

commands.explanation: Create a Docker context for remote daemon
commands.best_practice: Use contexts to separate environments (dev/stage/prod)

commands.explanation: Create and use buildx builder for multi-arch images
commands.use_case: Build images for amd64 and arm64
commands.best_practice: Cache build layers in CI and push to registry
Test multi-arch images on target platforms
Use short-lived tokens or credential stores
Implement image signing as part of release pipeline

commands.explanation: Inspect manifest list for multi-arch images
commands.best_practice: Use manifests to reference multi-arch images in orchestrators

commands.explanation: Copy files from container to host
commands.use_case: Retrieve logs or data from container
commands.best_practice: Prefer centralized logging instead of ad-hoc file copy in production

commands.explanation: Initialize Docker Swarm mode for clustering
commands.best_practice: Use Kubernetes for complex scheduling; Swarm for small footprint use cases
Prefer orchestrators with strong ecosystem for production

security: Run containers as non-root when possible
Scan images for vulnerabilities before deploying
scalability: Design stateless services for horizontal scaling
Externalize state to managed services or persistent volumes
monitoring: Export metrics from apps for scraping (Prometheus)
Collect container-level metrics and logs centrally
performance: Use multi-stage builds to reduce image size
Avoid large layers by combining commands carefully

common_errors_and_fixes.error: permission denied while trying to connect to the Docker daemon socket
common_errors_and_fixes.cause: User not in docker group or daemon not running
common_errors_and_fixes.fix: Add the user to docker group (newgrp docker) or start dockerd with systemctl
Use different host port or stop conflicting service
docker login to the registry or check image path

devops_integration.ci_cd: Use Docker in CI to build images, run tests inside containers, push artifacts to a registry, and trigger deployments to orchestration platforms.
devops_integration.monitoring_stack: Expose metrics, configure sidecars for logging, and add health checks to integrate with Prometheus/Grafana and ELK/Fluentd.
devops_integration.kubernetes: Use Docker images in Kubernetes deployments; follow image tagging and promotion practices to move images across environments.
devops_integration.automation: Automate image builds with BuildKit, sign images, and use deployment pipelines to promote images across environments.

beginner: How does Docker differ from a virtual machine?
intermediate: How do you manage secrets in containers?
senior: Design an image promotion workflow for multiple environments.
How would you secure a container supply chain?
Explain rootless Docker and its trade-offs.
scenario_based: You have an image causing high CPU on production—how do you investigate?
Design a zero-downtime deployment strategy using Docker images.

beginner: Containerize a static website and serve it with nginx
intermediate: Create a multi-container app with a database and web API using Docker Compose
production: Implement CI pipeline to build, scan, sign, and promote Docker images across dev->staging->prod

tips: Use .dockerignore to avoid sending build context files

intermediate: Learn multi-stage builds, networking, volumes, Compose
advanced: Image signing, rootless Docker, multi-arch builds, supply chain security
recommended_order: Linux basics -> Docker -> Docker Compose -> Kubernetes -> CI/CD

vulnerabilities: Use of outdated base images leading to CVEs
secure_configurations: Run as non-root, use read-only filesystem when feasible, use seccomp and AppArmor profiles
security.secrets_management: Avoid embedding secrets in images. Use secrets APIs from orchestrators, inject at runtime, or use secret managers.
security.container_security: Implement image scanning, runtime policies (e.g., OPA Gatekeeper), and minimal privileges.
security.rbac: When running in orchestrators, leverage platform RBAC to reduce blast radius of compromised credentials.

monitoring_observability.logs: Centralize container logs using a log forwarder (Fluentd/Fluent Bit) to send to ELK/Cloud logging.
monitoring_observability.metrics: Expose application metrics via Prometheus client and collect node/container metrics via cAdvisor or node exporters.
monitoring_observability.traces: Use distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) for request flows inside microservices.
monitoring_observability.alerting: Define SLI/SLOs and set alerts for high error rates, latency, resource exhaustion.
